burial.plot_notes: Center Cemetery, Shelby County Indiana — alongside wife Sarah Ann Edwards Huntington and parents.

narrative_blocks.header: Three-state migration before age five, Connecticut to Indiana
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Augustus Huntington was born August 26, 1816 in Coventry Connecticut, three years before his parents' 1819 westward move. The family went first to Lebanon Ohio, then about a year later to Connersville Indiana, and in 1821 finally settled 'in the wilds of Sugar Creek township' in Shelby County Indiana, where Henry grew up. The whole multi-step journey unfolded before he reached his fifth birthday.

narrative_blocks.header: Died exactly one month after wife Sarah
narrative_blocks.body: Henry and his wife Sarah Ann Edwards Huntington lived on the same Sugar Creek farm from 1852 until both died in 1895. Sarah died October 26, 1895; Henry died November 26, 1895 of paralysis — exactly one month later, to the day. They had been married forty-five years.

narrative_blocks.header: Thirteen-year Sugar Creek Township trustee
narrative_blocks.body: Henry served as Sugar Creek Township trustee for thirteen years — the longest single elected term in his community's documented late-nineteenth-century record. He was a Democrat and described in his 1895 obituary simply as 'an honest' man. The trustee role oversaw township roads, poor-relief, and school funding through the years of the agrarian populist movement that reshaped Indiana politics.

burial.plot_notes: Center Cemetery, Shelby County Indiana — alongside wife Sarah Ann Edwards Huntington and parents.

burial.plot_notes: Center Cemetery, Shelby County Indiana — alongside husband Henry and parents-in-law.

parents.father_research_notes: Edwards family of Miami County Ohio. Specific parents not yet identified. Son John Edwards Huntington (TD0277, 1861-1930) carries the surname forward.

narrative_blocks.header: Died exactly one month before husband Henry, 1895
narrative_blocks.body: Died October 26, 1895 at the Sugar Creek Township farm where she had lived with Henry since 1852 — exactly one month before Henry himself died of paralysis November 26, 1895. Mother of eleven children, eight of whom survived both parents.

death.cause: Heart attack per Roberta Huntington Cox 2008 obituary ('her father died of a heart attack').

burial.plot_notes: Luther Cemetery, Luther, Oklahoma County, Oklahoma — same cemetery as brother George B. Huntington (TD0278).

career.notes: Owned and operated the Luther Oklahoma general store with his wife Belle. Per Roberta's 2008 obit: 'Her parents owned and operated the general store in Luther, and their three children were cared for by two loving aunts, Lizza and Amanda, while their parents were at the store.' The 'Amanda' caregiver-aunt likely refers to George B's wife Amanda Ferris Huntington (I01150).

death.cause: Stroke per Roberta's 2008 obituary ('her mother, Belle, who had suffered a stroke').

parents.father_research_notes: Sellman family. Parents not identified. No documented Hooker/Talcott connection.

career.notes: Appointed Postmistress of Luther OK post office. Per Roberta's 2008 obituary: 'Her mother, Belle, had received an appointment to be Postmistress of the Luther post office, and Roberta became the Assistant postmistress.' Roberta continued in the Assistant position until 1974.
Co-owned and co-operated the Luther OK general store with husband John Edwards Huntington until his 1930 death.

narrative_blocks.header: Postmistress of Luther Oklahoma after widowhood
narrative_blocks.body: Widowed 1930 when husband John died of heart attack at age sixty-eight or sixty-nine. Appointed Postmistress of Luther Oklahoma post office; daughter Roberta Huntington Cox became her Assistant Postmistress and stayed forty-one years. Lived another forty-seven years to age ninety-seven before her 1977 stroke and death — outliving John by nearly half a century.

burial.plot_notes: Memorial Park Cemetery, Oklahoma City — alongside husband Luther 'Luke' Cox.

education.notes: Texas Women's College in Denton TX. Attended starting 1927; left when her father John Edwards Huntington died of heart attack 1930. Returned to Luther OK.
Business College after returning to Oklahoma post-father's death.

career.notes: Worked for the Superintendent of Schools in Oklahoma City after business college.

Assistant Postmistress at Luther OK post office — her mother Belle Sellman Huntington (I01153) was the appointed Postmistress. Roberta served in the assistant role for forty-one years until her 1974 retirement.
After her 1974 retirement Roberta became the full-time caregiver for her mother Belle after Belle's stroke. Cared for Belle until Belle's 1977 (or 1978) death.

narrative_blocks.header: Born and died November 22, ninety-nine years apart
narrative_blocks.body: Born November 22, 1909 in Luther Oklahoma and died November 22, 2008 in Tulsa Oklahoma — exactly on her ninety-ninth birthday. Her sixty-seven-year marriage to Luther 'Luke' Cox ended with his November 2000 death, after which she moved to Tulsa to be near her late brother Ralph's daughters Mary Lynn and Jo Ann.

narrative_blocks.header: Assistant Postmistress at Luther Oklahoma forty-one years
narrative_blocks.body: Worked as Assistant Postmistress at the Luther Oklahoma post office for forty-one years until her 1974 retirement — her mother Belle had been the appointed Postmistress. After retirement Roberta became Belle's full-time caregiver until her mother's 1977 stroke and death.

burial.plot_notes: Luther Cemetery, Luther, Oklahoma County, Oklahoma.

narrative_blocks.header: Indiana to Oklahoma: lived to age ninety-three
narrative_blocks.body: Born November 8, 1863 Sugar Creek Township Shelby County Indiana to Henry Augustus + Sarah Ann Edwards Huntington. Died December 13, 1956 at Edmond Oklahoma age ninety-three — a final Huntington migration west, from the Indiana frontier his grandfather settled in 1821 to the Oklahoma plains.

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed at forty-five with surviving children
narrative_blocks.body: Married Amanda Ferris March 23, 1887; she died July 22, 1908 at Shelbyville Indiana age forty after twenty-one years of marriage and six children — three of whom predeceased her. Three surviving children (Abbie, Ben Ferris, George Frederick Sr.) carried the line forward, with the youngest born only six years before his mother's death.

burial.plot_notes: Center Cemetery, Shelby County Indiana — alongside Huntington in-laws (her father-in-law Henry Augustus and mother-in-law Sarah Ann Edwards).

parents.father_research_notes: Ferris family, New York. Specific parents not identified.

narrative_blocks.header: Died age forty, having buried three children
narrative_blocks.body: Born November 8, 1867 New York; married George B. Huntington in Indiana 1887 at age nineteen. Bore six children between 1888 and 1902, three of whom died young (Walter 1893 infant; Olive age six in 1900; Ruth age two in 1900).

burial.plot_notes: Memorial Park Cemetery, Oklahoma City, Oklahoma County, Oklahoma — alongside wife Louisa and daughter Dorothy.

narrative_blocks.header: Tied his brother George B at age ninety-two
narrative_blocks.body: Born November 16, 1865 Sugar Creek Township Shelby County Indiana; died 1958 at Norman Oklahoma age ninety-two — tied with his older brother George B. Huntington as the longest-lived of the eight Henry Augustus Huntington children. Both brothers followed the family migration west from Indiana to Oklahoma; both lived into their nineties.

bio.extra_names: Maiden + married surname both Huntington — possible cousin marriage

burial.plot_notes: Memorial Park Cemetery, Oklahoma City, Oklahoma — alongside husband Charles William and daughter Dorothy.

parents.father_research_notes: Huntington family of Boggstown Shelby County Indiana. Possibly a documented cousin to her husband Charles William Huntington (TD0279) — both born in Shelby County IN, both surnamed Huntington at birth. If confirmed via family research, would establish a cousin_marriage CC pattern. Specific parents not yet identified.

burial.plot_notes: Memorial Park Cemetery, Oklahoma City — alongside parents.
Summit View Cemetery, Guthrie, Logan County, Oklahoma — alongside wife Gertrude.

bio.extra_names: Maiden + married surname both Huntington — possible distant relation

burial.plot_notes: Summit View Cemetery, Guthrie, Logan County, Oklahoma.

parents.father_research_notes: Huntington family of Kansas. Less obvious cousin connection than Louisa Huntington Huntington (I01151, born Shelby Co IN same county as her husband's family). Could be unrelated Huntington family or Kansas branch. Specific parents not identified.

burial.plot_notes: Center Cemetery, Shelby County Indiana — pioneer-era family cemetery; same cemetery as wife Mary Tyler Morse Huntington (I01146) and son Henry Augustus Huntington (TD0263).

career.notes: Per Huntington Family in America 1915 record: 'He had spent some portion of his life on the sea.' Specific maritime career details not documented.

Removed from Connecticut to Shelby County Indiana 1819 — joining the first Yankee wave to the newly-admitted state (Indiana statehood 1816). 'A man honored by his fellow citizens with frequent testimonials of their confidence' per the Huntington Family in America 1915 record. Died at his Shelby County residence July 20, 1844.

narrative_blocks.header: Removed 1819 from Connecticut to Shelby County Indiana
narrative_blocks.body: Removed from Connecticut to Shelby County Indiana in 1819 — joining the first Yankee wave to the newly-admitted state (Indiana statehood 1816). His six children all born between 1811 and 1825 followed him west; he died at his Shelby County residence July 20, 1844.

narrative_blocks.header: Spent some portion of his life at sea
narrative_blocks.body: Per the Huntington Family in America 1915 record: 'He had spent some portion of his life on the sea' before settling in Indiana as a respected citizen. Honored by fellow citizens with frequent testimonials of their confidence in his character.

burial.plot_notes: Center Cemetery, Shelby County Indiana — alongside husband Septimus.

parents.father_research_notes: Morse family of Wrentham (Norfolk County) MA. Wrentham was the same town as the Morses (Samuel F.B. Morse the telegraph inventor's family). Specific parents not yet identified.

death.cause: Complication of diseases per contemporary account

education.notes: Yale College B.A. 1762. Entered at age 23 after his father had destined him for the clothier trade against his own wishes; his pastor Rev. Ebenezer Devotion encouraged the late preparation. Graduated honorably.

career.notes: Installed June 29, 1763 — within one year of his Yale graduation. Continued as pastor until his death thirty-one years later. Under his ministry the parish built the best meeting house in the region for $5,000 — services at his ordination were held in the open air either because the existing meeting house was too small or too dilapidated.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College 1762; D.D. honoris causa 1780; Trustee 1780-1788.

notable.notable_blurb: Coventry minister who posthumously published Universalism

cross_connections.display_label: Tutored Nathan Hale at Coventry for Yale entrance

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter burned most copies of his Universalist treatise
narrative_blocks.body: 'Calvinism Improved' — his posthumous 1796 treatise defending universal salvation and denying eternal punishment — was doomed to limited circulation because one of his daughters consigned the greater part of the edition to the flames. Sprague's Annals of the American Pulpit notes 'so effectual was the suppression of the work that it is now almost impossible to find a copy.'

narrative_blocks.header: Son from first marriage became Ohio Governor
narrative_blocks.body: His eldest son with Hannah Devotion — Samuel H. Huntington — was orphaned at age six when Hannah died in 1771 and was adopted by his uncle Samuel Huntington, the Connecticut Governor and Declaration signer. The boy grew up to attend Dartmouth and Yale, settled Cleveland in 1801, and became the third Governor of Ohio 1808-1810 — half-brother to the nine children Joseph would later have with second wife Elizabeth Hale.

narrative_blocks.header: Tutored Nathan Hale of Coventry for Yale
narrative_blocks.body: He prepared Nathan Hale (1755-1776) of Coventry for Yale before Hale entered the college in 1769 and graduated 1773. Hale was hanged at New York September 22, 1776 — 'I only regret that I have but one life to lose for my country.' Both Huntington and Hale are buried at the Nathan Hale Cemetery in Coventry.

narrative_blocks.header: Almost succeeded Wheelock as Dartmouth president, 1780
narrative_blocks.body: After the death of Rev Eleazar Wheelock — Dartmouth's first president — communications were made to Huntington that gave him reason to expect election as Wheelock's successor. He actually made the journey to Hanover NH before declining. Yale conferred a D.D. honoris causa on him that same year, and he was elected a Yale Trustee 1780-1788.

narrative_blocks.header: Preached Universalism quietly for two decades
narrative_blocks.body: Old parishioners later claimed 'Dr Huntington preached good Universalism for twenty years, but in such a way that he was not suspected, except by those who relished it.' His preface to Calvinism Improved acknowledged the work 'contained a small part of a system of divinity which the author has been meditating more than twenty years' — his Coventry parish never knew their pastor's actual theology.

narrative_blocks.header: Open-air ordination at decayed Coventry meeting house
narrative_blocks.body: Installed June 29, 1763 as pastor of the First Church of Coventry, where the previous meeting house had been suffered to go to decay. The ordination services were held in the open air, whether because the building was too small or too dilapidated to be safe never made clear. Within a few years he led the parish to build the best house of public worship in the region at a cost of five thousand dollars.

marriages.notes: Stage 6Y-202: eldest surviving child TD0238 built (Elizabeth Hale Huntington Jones, m. Amasa Jones 1794, → Wilkes-Barre PA 1816). 8 other M2 children deferred (Talcott gen 6 cohort, mostly died young): Septimus 1773-1776, George Washington Huntington 1776-1777, Septimus George 1778-1844, Hannah 1779-1794, Henry 1781-1806, Lucretia Norton 1783-1876, Mercy Penelope 1788-1794, James 1790-1793.

narrative_blocks.header: Father died of typhus at Siege of Boston
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Capt Jonathan Hale Jr died of camp fever at Roxbury MA on March 7, 1776 — less than four years after her August 1772 marriage to Joseph Huntington. Her older brother Jonathan died unmarried that October and her younger sister Jerusha died that September, leaving her mother widowed with three children buried in one Revolutionary year.

narrative_blocks.header: Six of nine children predeceased her
narrative_blocks.body: Of the nine children she bore Rev Joseph Huntington between 1773 and 1790, six died before she did — Septimus (age 3), George Washington (age 1), Hannah (age 15), Penelope (age 6), James (age 3), and Henry (age 25 same year as her own death). Only Elizabeth, Septimus George, and Lucretia outlived their mother to maturity.
